title: ARIMA latent residuals: maths, priors, and usage
text: vignette covers maths behind epinowcast’s ARIMA(p, d, q) latent residual terms, use module’s formula, set priors. explains kernel construction, stationarity parameterisation, regularisation choices, trade-offs made explicit. worked usage comparison rw() lme4-style random effects, see latent processes vignette. full model definition see model vignette.

title: ARIMA latent residuals: maths, priors, and usage
text: arima(time, , p, d, q) term formula declares latent residual series added relevant linear predictor every observation. Writing linear predictor module \(\eta_i\) observation \(\) time \(t_i\) group \(g_i\): \[\eta_i = X_i \beta + Z_i b + \epsilon_{t_i, g_i},\] \(X_i \beta\) fixed effects, \(Z_i b\) random effects (including rw() contributions, handled design matrix), \(\epsilon_{t_i, g_i}\) value ARIMA latent series time \(t_i\) group \(g_i\). residual evolves time axis declared arima(...); observation just looks value (time, group) index. construction brms uses cor_arma-style residual correlation non-Gaussian likelihoods: latent Gaussian residual added linear predictor, ARMA structure imposed prior.

what: Using ARIMA terms in a formula
title: ARIMA latent residuals: maths, priors, and usage
text: Add term module’s formula arima() one aliases, build backend term: rw(time) — random walk, equal arima(time, p = 0, d = 1, q = 0). ar(time, p) — stationary autoregression, equal arima(time, p, d = 0, q = 0). ma(time, q) arma(time, p, q) — moving-average ARMA aliases. arima(time, , p, d, q) — general form. time column series evolves , optional gives group shocks shared dynamics. latent processes vignette works growth rate compares rw() lme4-style random effects; Priors section covers tuning regularisation.

title: ARIMA latent residuals: maths, priors, and usage
text: Let \(T\) number time points series \(G\) number groups. ARIMA latent built \(T \times G\) matrix unit-normal shocks \(z\) via \[\epsilon = \sigma \, K(\phi, \theta, d) \, z,\] \(\sigma\) latent standard deviation \(K\) \(T \times T\) lower-triangular kernel encodes ARIMA structure. kernel factorises \[K(\phi, \theta, d) = D^d \, T(\psi),\] : \(\psi \\mathbb{R}^T\) truncated impulse response ARMA(p, q) process autoregressive coefficients \(\phi\) moving-average coefficients \(\theta\). satisfies \(\psi_1 = 1\) standard ARMA recursion[1] \[\psi_k = \sum_{j=1}^{\min(p, k-1)} \phi_j \psi_{k-j} + \mathbb{1}\{k - 1 \le q\} \, \theta_{k-1}, \quad k = 2, \dots, T.\] \(T(\psi) \\mathbb{R}^{T \times T}\) lower-triangular Toeplitz matrix first column \(\psi\). Multiplying \(T(\psi) z\) applies ARMA filter shocks: entry \((T(\psi) z)_t = \sum_{k=1}^{t} \psi_{t-k+1} z_k\). \(D \\mathbb{R}^{T \times T}\) lower-triangular cumulative-sum operator, ones diagonal. \(D^d\) integrates \(d\) times. \(D^0 = \) leaves ARMA series unchanged; \(D^1\) produces integrated series order one; higher \(d\) generalise. Two properties factorisation useful: \(D^d\) data-. can precomputed contribute autodiff graph Stan. \(T(\psi)\) parameter-dependent. rebuilt every gradient evaluation, built globally applied shocks single matrix multiplication. Composing two applying shocks gives full ARIMA(p, d, q) latent: Stan implementation steps lives inst/stan/functions/arima_kernel.stan.
code: psi  <- arma_impulse(phi, theta, T)        # impulse response, length T T_K  <- lower_toeplitz(psi)                # T x T ARMA kernel D_d  <- cumulative_op(T, d)                # T x T integration operator K    <- D_d * T_K                          # T x T composed kernel eps  <- sigma * K * z                      # T x G latent

title: ARIMA latent residuals: maths, priors, and usage
text: autoregressive part well behaved stationary. stationary AR(p) process forgets past, impulse response decays latent series stays bounded; non-stationary one opposite, impulse response grows without bound sampler diverges. Putting prior directly \(\phi\) awkward stationary region \(\phi\)-space irregular shape hard sample inside. sidestep standard reparameterisation. Instead \(\phi\) work partial autocorrelations \(r_1, \dots, r_p\), free range \((-1, 1)\). Every \(r\) corresponds exactly one stationary \(\phi\) vice versa, sampling \(r\) simple box \((-1, 1)^p\) stays stationary region construction. map \(r\) \(\phi\) Durbin-Levinson recursion — recursion classically used fit AR models sample autocorrelations, run forward direction[2]. \(k = 1, \dots, p\), starting \(\phi_1^{(1)} = r_1\), \[\phi_k^{(k)} = r_k, \quad \phi_j^{(k)} = \phi_j^{(k-1)} - r_k \phi_{k-j}^{(k-1)}, \quad j = 1, \dots, k-1,\] final coefficients \(\phi_j = \phi_j^{(p)}\). construction roots \(1 - \phi_1 z - \dots - \phi_p z^p\) lie outside unit circle, exactly stationarity condition. approach brms rstanarm use AR(p) priors. Stan declare vector<lower=-1, upper=1>[p] expr_arima_pacf directly, transform \(\phi\) via pacf_to_phi(pacf) inside transformed parameters / model block. bounds put implicit uniform prior \(r_k\) \((-1, 1)\); override priors mechanism needed. MA invertibility enforced explicitly. typical values rarely sampling problem; becomes one specific use case, partial-autocorrelation transform applies symmetrically \(\theta\). kernel produces \(\epsilon \\mathbb{R}^{T \times G}\), observations live flat vector. observation carries time group index, contribution linear predictor single matrix lookup per observation — negligible next building kernel.

title: ARIMA latent residuals: maths, priors, and usage
text: supplied group column unit-normal shocks z[, g], AR/MA parameters \(\phi\), \(\theta\), latent standard deviation \(\sigma\) shared across groups. kernel \(K\) built applied column \(T \times G\) shock matrix one matrix multiplication, group’s series driven innovations common dynamic. Per-group parameters (group \(\phi\), \(\theta\), \(\sigma\)) planned extension loop \(g\) building per-group \(K_g\); lands helpers expose type argument.

title: ARIMA latent residuals: maths, priors, and usage
text: factorisation makes several familiar models special cases code path: arima(time, p = 0, d = 0, q = 0) degenerate rejected constructor. arima(time, p = 0, d = 1, q = 0) reduces random walk: \(\psi = (1, 0, 0, \dots)\), \(T(\psi) = \) \(K = D\) cumulative-sum operator. rw(time) now thin user-facing wrapper constructs exactly term; go backend produce identical posteriors. pure-differencing case (AR MA structure) detected arima_filter() short-circuited per-column cumulative_sum() kernel never materialised — cost \(O(T G)\) per evaluation, previous bespoke random-walk implementation. arima(time, p = 1, d = 0, q = 0) stationary AR(1): \(\psi_k = \phi^{k-1}\), \(T(\psi)\) standard AR(1) Toeplitz, \(K = T(\psi)\). arima(time, p = 0, d = 1, q = 1) integrated MA(1) (random-walk--correlated-shocks model commonly seen epidemiology). arima_kernel_matrix() function unit-tested cases stats::ARMAtoMA direct R reference recursions; see tests/testthat/test-stan_arima_kernel.R.

title: ARIMA latent residuals: maths, priors, and usage
text: default priors deliberately weak. latent scale \(\sigma\) partial autocorrelations \(r_k\) can overridden standard priors argument relevant module; shocks MA terms fixed. \(z_{t,g} \sim \mathcal{N}(0, 1)\) — fixed non-centred parameterisation; user-tunable. \(\sigma \sim \mathcal{N}_+(0, 0.2)\) (half-normal) — controls strongly latent series can deviate zero. Set <prefix>_arima_sigma prior. \(r_k \sim \mathrm{Uniform}(-1, 1)\) — default, implicit parameter bounds. Set <prefix>_arima_pacf prior: positive standard deviation switches \(\mathcal{N}(\mu, s)\) prior truncated \((-1, 1)\), shrinking autocorrelations toward \(\mu\). standard deviation zero keeps uniform default. \(\theta_l \sim \mathcal{N}(0, 1)\) MA terms present — weakly informative. <prefix> module term sits (expr growth rate, refp parametric reference mean, ; see can use ). example, shrink growth-rate AR term toward weak autocorrelation rather leaving uniform: half-normal prior \(\sigma\) consequential practice; tighten latent residual competing rest linear predictor variance. tighter pacf prior lever discouraging near-unit-root AR behaviour uniform default permits.
code: library(epinowcast) pacf_prior <- data.frame(variable = "expr_arima_pacf", mean = 0, sd = 0.3) # epinowcast(..., priors = pacf_prior)

title: ARIMA latent residuals: maths, priors, and usage
text: arima() aliases work every module takes formula, prior prefix: enw_expectation() — growth rate (expr) latent--obs proportion (expl). enw_reference() — parametric mean (refp) non-parametric logit hazards (refnp). enw_report() — report-date logit hazards (rep). enw_missing() — missing-reference proportion (miss). Putting arima() term reference delay mean, instance, models reporting delay drifts time, arima() growth rate models epidemic trend .

title: ARIMA latent residuals: maths, priors, and usage
text: One ARIMA term per formula. R side aborts multiple supplied; lifting requires Stan side gain vector latent series rather single one. AR stationarity enforced; MA invertibility (rarely problem practice). Grouped series share AR/MA parameters latent standard deviation across groups; per-group parameters planned extension. lands arima() rw() take type argument. kernel built dense (\(T \times T\)) every iteration. long series (thousands time points) autodiff cost grows \(O(T^2)\); regime recursive form (computing \(\epsilon\) via ARMA filter directly) preferable. parametric reference standard deviation modelled (model_refp > 1), shares ARIMA shocks kernel mean carries independent scale (refp_arima_sd_sigma). two residuals therefore perfectly correlated scale rather driven separate shock series. separate sd process planned extension.

title: Estimating reporting delays with the full and delay-only models
text: vignette estimates delays aggregate reporting triangles. individual (line list) data, epidist estimates delay distributions directly primary secondary event times. epidist delay-model build primarycensored, provides primary-event-censored, truncated distributions underpin correct delay estimation; main difference approaches data structure expect (aggregate counts versus individual event times epidist). EpiNow2::estimate_dist() function fills similar role delay estimation ecosystem.

what: Two ways to estimate a delay
title: Estimating reporting delays with the full and delay-only models
text: full model treats cell reporting triangle noisy observation expected value built latent process delay distribution. delay identified jointly latent process, recent reference dates uncertainty latent process propagates delay. delay-model takes total reference date fixed truth models total split across delays. reference date total \(N_t\) delay probabilities \(p_d\) observed cells \(n_{t,d}\) follow multinomial, \[ n_{t, 0:D} \mid N_t \sim \mathrm{Multinomial}(N_t, p_{0:D}), \] standard conditional delay likelihood[1,2]. delays horizon observed probabilities renormalised observed range, giving truncated multinomial. removes latent-process identification burden gives tighter delay estimates recent dates, cost assuming totals correct. delay-model combined missing reference date module (enw_missing()): conditions known totals fully referenced cells, separate missing-reference stream model.

title: Estimating reporting delays with the full and delay-only models
Estimating reporting delays with the full and delay-only models
text: simulate hospitalisations known lognormal reporting delay can check model recovers truth. reference date known total, cells multinomial draw total across delays using delay PMF. use modest number reference dates modest total per date posterior visible uncertainty rather collapsing point. take true delay PMF primarycensored::dprimarycensored(), primary-event-censored discretisation epinowcast uses, simulated truth matches model’s parameterisation.
code: library(primarycensored) set.seed(890)  meanlog <- 1.6 sdlog <- 0.5 max_delay <- 15 n_dates <- 30 total <- 200  delays <- 0:(max_delay - 1) delay_pmf <- dprimarycensored(   delays, pdist = plnorm, pwindow = 1, swindow = 1, D = max_delay,   meanlog = meanlog, sdlog = sdlog )  dates <- as.Date("2021-01-01") + 0:(n_dates - 1)  obs <- rbindlist(lapply(seq_along(dates), function(i) {   counts <- as.vector(rmultinom(1, total, delay_pmf))   data.table(     reference_date = dates[i],     report_date = dates[i] + delays,     confirm = cumsum(counts)   ) }))  pobs <- enw_preprocess_data(obs, max_delay = max_delay)

title: Estimating reporting delays with the full and delay-only models
code: full_fit <- epinowcast(   pobs,   reference = enw_reference(~1, distribution = "lognormal", data = pobs),   obs = enw_obs(family = "poisson", data = pobs),   fit = enw_fit_opts(     save_warmup = FALSE, chains = 2,     iter_warmup = 500, iter_sampling = 500,     show_messages = FALSE, refresh = 0   ) )

title: Estimating reporting delays with the full and delay-only models
text: delay-model uses reference-delay specification sets delay_only = TRUE enw_obs(). delay-likelihood multinomial conditional known totals, observation family needed. expectation module configure: known totals override expected observations, epinowcast() minimises (now inert) expectation automatically.
code: delay_fit <- epinowcast(   pobs,   reference = enw_reference(~1, distribution = "lognormal", data = pobs),   obs = enw_obs(delay_only = TRUE, data = pobs),   fit = enw_fit_opts(     nowcast = FALSE, save_warmup = FALSE, chains = 2,     iter_warmup = 500, iter_sampling = 500,     show_messages = FALSE, refresh = 0   ) )

title: Estimating reporting delays with the full and delay-only models
text: models recover simulated lognormal parameters (meanlog = 1.6, sdlog = 0.5). compare posterior actual distribution parameters truth. posterior means sit close simulated truth models, truth lies within 90% credible interval, demonstrating recovery.
code: pars <- c("refp_mean_int", "refp_sd_int") truth <- data.table(   variable = c("refp_mean_int[1]", "refp_sd_int[1]"),   truth = c(meanlog, sdlog) )  param_summary <- rbind(   data.table(model = "full", full_fit$fit[[1]]$summary(pars)),   data.table(model = "delay-only", delay_fit$fit[[1]]$summary(pars)) ) param_summary <- merge(   param_summary, truth, by = "variable" )[, .(model, variable, truth, mean, q5, q95)] knitr::kable(param_summary, digits = 3)

title: Estimating reporting delays with the full and delay-only models
text: use enw_posterior_delay() extract posterior samples delay PMF fit plot truth, rather posterior mean. Posterior samples delay distribution model truth coloured line one posterior draw, spread lines posterior uncertainty delay distribution. draws bracket simulated truth (dashed) models, recover delay; delay-posterior (tighter band) carry latent-process uncertainty.
code: full_pmf <- enw_posterior_delay(   full_fit$fit[[1]], max_delay = max_delay, draws = 100 )[, model := "full"] delay_pmf_draws <- enw_posterior_delay(   delay_fit$fit[[1]], max_delay = max_delay, draws = 100 )[, model := "delay-only"]  truth_dt <- data.table(delay = delays, pmf = delay_pmf)  ggplot() +   geom_line(     data = rbind(full_pmf, delay_pmf_draws),     aes(x = delay, y = pmf, group = interaction(model, .draw), colour = model),     alpha = 0.1   ) +   geom_line(     data = truth_dt, aes(x = delay, y = pmf), colour = "black",     linewidth = 1, linetype = "dashed"   ) +   labs(     x = "Delay", y = "Probability", colour = NULL,     caption = "Dashed black line is the simulated truth."   ) +   theme_bw()

what: Recovering the delay from data with missing cells
title: Estimating reporting delays with the full and delay-only models
text: Real reporting triangles often gaps: delay cells never observed even though earlier later ones . delay-model handles observation indicator (.observed column), renormalising delays observation cutoff interior cells unobserved cutoff still contribute. mask delays 3 6 every reference date confirm delay still recovered. Even masked interior cells delay recovered, cells keep weight renormalisation rather dropped.
code: comp <- enw_complete_dates(obs, flag_observation = TRUE) comp[, .observed :=   .observed & !(as.integer(report_date - reference_date) %in% c(3L, 6L))] pobs_missing <- enw_preprocess_data(comp, max_delay = max_delay)  missing_fit <- epinowcast(   pobs_missing,   reference = enw_reference(~1, distribution = "lognormal", data = pobs_missing),   obs = enw_obs(     delay_only = TRUE,     observation_indicator = ".observed", data = pobs_missing   ),   fit = enw_fit_opts(     nowcast = FALSE, save_warmup = FALSE, chains = 2,     iter_warmup = 500, iter_sampling = 500,     show_messages = FALSE, refresh = 0   ) ) missing_summary <- merge(   data.table(missing_fit$fit[[1]]$summary(pars)), truth, by = "variable" )[, .(variable, truth, mean, q5, q95)] knitr::kable(missing_summary, digits = 3)

title: Discretised distributions
text: currently available parametric delay distributions continuous probability distributions () two parameters \(\mu_{g,t}\) \(\upsilon_{g,t}\). table provides link definition distribution, specifies parameters \(\mu_{g,t}\) \(\upsilon_{g,t}\) mapped parameters distribution (according referenced definition), states resulting mean distribution (discretization adjustment assumed maximum delay). log-logistic distribution previously available dropped pending log-logistic support primarycensored (epinowcast/primarycensored#321).

what: Discretisation and adjustment for maximum delay
title: Discretised distributions
text: epinowcast, delays modelled discrete time assumed maximum delay (specified via max_delay argument). continuous delay distributions must therefore discretised adjusted maximum delay. helpful separate two distinct adjustments. first discretisation: turning continuous delay probability mass integer delays \(d = 0, 1, 2, \dots\), \(p_d\) defined infinite maximum delay \(\sum_{d=0}^{\infty} p_d = 1\). second conditioning maximum delay \(D\): restricting attention delays \(d \le D\) renormalising truncated probabilities sum 1, .e. \(p^{\prime}_{d} = p_d / \sum_{j=0}^{D} p_j\). first step continuous distribution becomes discrete; second right truncation \(D\).

previous_headings: Discretisation and adjustment for maximum delay
title: Discretised distributions
text: epinowcast discretises parametric reference delay using double interval censoring approach primarycensored package[1]. accounts primary event window, secondary (reporting) interval, right truncation maximum delay \(D\). Let \(F^{\mu_{g,t}, \upsilon_{g,t}}\) cumulative distribution function continuous delay distribution. primary event (e.g. infection) observed exactly assumed uniform window width 1 day. Censoring continuous delay primary window gives \[Q(t) = \int_0^1 F^{\mu_{g,t}, \upsilon_{g,t}}(t - s) \, \mathrm{d}s,\] cumulative probability delay, measured start primary window, \(t\). secondary event observed daily reporting interval, mass integer delay \(d\) increment \(Q\) interval, conditioned maximum delay \(D\), \[p_{g,t,d} = \frac{Q(d + 1) - Q(d)}{Q(D)}, \qquad d = 0, 1, \dots, D - 1.\] denominator \(Q(D)\) applies right truncation, discretised probabilities sum 1. primarycensored evaluates \(Q\) analytical solutions supported distributions (exponential handled gamma shape one), Stan implementation vendored directly package. applied automatically available parametric distributions (lognormal, gamma exponential); argument needed. See primarycensored documentation full derivation, including arbitrary primary secondary window widths. discretised mass function lognormal delay (meanlog = 1, sdlog = 0.5) truncated maximum delay 15 days, obtained directly primarycensored::dprimarycensored():  primarycensored machinery underpins delay handling elsewhere ecosystem. epidist estimates delay distributions individual line-list data, EpiNow2::estimate_dist() fits aggregated count data; powered primarycensored, main difference data structure expect. Estimating delay one tools passing enw_reference() keeps censoring assumptions consistent across workflow.
code: dmax <- 15 pmf <- data.table(   delay = 0:(dmax - 1),   probability = primarycensored::dprimarycensored(     0:(dmax - 1), plnorm, pwindow = 1, swindow = 1, D = dmax,     meanlog = 1, sdlog = 0.5   ) )  ggplot(pmf, aes(x = delay, y = probability)) +   geom_col(fill = "#3182bd") +   labs(     x = "Delay (days)", y = "Probability",     title = "Discretised lognormal delay (meanlog = 1, sdlog = 0.5)"   ) +   theme_bw()

code: plot(pobs_full, type = "delay_cumulative") pobs <- enw_preprocess_data(   retro_nat_germany, max_delay = 28 ) pobs #> ── Preprocessed nowcast data ───────────────────────────────────────────────────  #> Groups: 1 | Timestep: day | Max delay: 28  #> Observations: 40 timepoints x 40 snapshots  #> Max date: 2021-08-22  #>  #> Datasets (access with `enw_get_data(x, "<name>")`):  #>   obs                :     742 x 9  #>   new_confirm        :     742 x 11  #>   latest             :      40 x 10  #>   missing_reference  :       0 x 6  #>   reporting_triangle :      40 x 30  #>   metareference      :      40 x 9  #>   metareport         :      67 x 12  #>   metadelay          :      28 x 5

title: Getting Started with Epinowcast: Nowcasting
text: epinowcast package provides modular modelling framework. package comes equipped several modules can combine construct models, also allows us create modules. ensures models can tailored specific data context. module defines default priors can inspected customised. See ?epinowcast details inspecting setting priors. commonly used process model nowcasting model expected counts date reference via geometric random walk. acts minimally informed smoothing prior gives lot weight observed data. default process model epinowcast. default model also uses lognormal delay distribution report-date effects. historically common specification field tuned particular problem. plot chunk nowcast-default
code: model <- enw_model() options(mc.cores = 2) fit_opts <- enw_fit_opts(   save_warmup = FALSE,   pp = TRUE,   chains = 2,   iter_sampling = 500,   iter_warmup = 500,   adapt_delta = 0.9,   show_messages = interactive() ) nowcast_default <- epinowcast(   data = pobs,   fit = fit_opts,   model = model ) nowcast_default #> ── epinowcast model output ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────  #> Groups: 1 | Timestep: day | Max delay: 28  #> Observations: 40 timepoints x 40 snapshots  #> Max date: 2021-08-22  #>  #> Datasets (access with `enw_get_data(x, "<name>")`):  #>   obs                :     742 x 9  #>   new_confirm        :     742 x 11  #>   latest             :      40 x 10  #>   missing_reference  :       0 x 6  #>   reporting_triangle :      40 x 30  #>   metareference      :      40 x 9  #>   metareport         :      67 x 12  #>   metadelay          :      28 x 5  #>  #> Model objects (access with `enw_get_data(x, "<name>")`):  #>   priors : 14 x 6  #>   fit : CmdStanMCMC  #>   data : list(115)  #>   fit_args : list(7)  #>   init_method_output : NULL  #> Model fit:  #>   Samples: 500 | Max Rhat: 1.05  #>   Divergent transitions: 0 (0%)  #>   Max treedepth: 8 (118 at max, 23.6%)  #>   Run time: 17.1 secs plot(   nowcast_default,   latest_obs = latest_germany_hosp )

title: Getting Started with Epinowcast: Nowcasting
text: underlying process model exponential growth rate model (\(C_t = C_{t-1} \exp^{r_t}\)). capture sustained upward trend, specify weekly random walk growth rate baseline intercept. also add day--week random effect account weekly periodicity visible data. intercept provides baseline growth rate, rw(week) allows change week without overfitting daily noise. (1 | day_of_week) term captures weekly seasonal pattern.
code: expectation_module <- enw_expectation(   ~ 1 + rw(week) + (1 | day_of_week),   data = pobs )

title: Getting Started with Epinowcast: Nowcasting
text: default model assumes lognormal delay distribution fixed parameters. first alternative model, keep lognormal distribution add partially pooled day--week effects mean standard deviation. second alternative model, replace non-parametric specification makes distributional assumption. Instead, estimates baseline reporting hazard daily random walk delays day--week random effect reference date, allowing hazard vary smoothly delays weekday case tested.
code: lognormal_reference <- enw_reference(   parametric = ~ 1 + (1 | day_of_week),   distribution = "lognormal",   data = pobs ) np_reference <- enw_reference(   parametric = ~0,   non_parametric = ~ 1 + rw(delay) + (1 | day_of_week),   data = pobs )

title: Getting Started with Epinowcast: Nowcasting
text: keep default negative binomial observation model (family = "negbin", quadratic mean-variance relationship). enw_obs() also provides "negbin1d" (linear mean-variance), can preferable noise scales linearly mean nowcasts summed coarser time units, since NB1 additive aggregation NB2 . reuse shared fit_opts alternative models. Since Stan model already compiled, sampling time required.
code: nowcast_lognormal <- epinowcast(   data = pobs,   expectation = expectation_module,   reference = lognormal_reference,   fit = fit_opts,   model = model ) nowcast_np <- epinowcast(   data = pobs,   expectation = expectation_module,   reference = np_reference,   fit = fit_opts,   model = model )

text: can compare three nowcasts overlaying posterior summaries. plot chunk comparison default model captures general level hospitalisations underestimates recent dates misses day--week pattern. alternative models better capture upward trend weekly periodicity. two alternative models produce similar estimates dataset, delay distribution approximately lognormal. non-parametric model shows slightly wider uncertainty recent dates, reflecting additional flexibility daily delay random walk. settings delay distribution multi-modal irregular, non-parametric approach show larger advantage. extensions consider include: Combining parametric non-parametric components single reference model balance flexibility parsimony. Allowing delay distribution report effects vary time reporting process changes. Stratifying age group location using hierarchical models. Formally comparing models using leave-one-cross-validation scoring rules.
code: summaries <- list(   Default = nowcast_default,   Lognormal = nowcast_lognormal,   `Non-parametric` = nowcast_np ) combined <- rbindlist(   lapply(names(summaries), function(name) {     out <- summary(summaries[[name]], probs = c(0.05, 0.2, 0.8, 0.95))     out[, model := name][]   }),   use.names = TRUE )  enw_plot_nowcast_quantiles(   combined, latest_obs = latest_germany_hosp ) +   facet_wrap(vars(model))

title: Gaussian process latent terms: maths, priors, and usage
text: vignette covers maths behind epinowcast’s Gaussian process latent terms, use module’s formula, set priors. explains Hilbert-space spectral approximation, available kernels, regularisation choices, trade-offs made explicit. worked usage comparison rw(), arima(), lme4-style random effects, see latent processes vignette. full model definition see model vignette. implementation ported EpiNow2 (epiforecasts/EpiNow2, MIT licensed), Hilbert-space approximate Gaussian process used default latent process reproduction number. Stan functions inst/stan/functions/gaussian_process.stan carry attribution comment effect. maths follows EpiNow2 implementation notes underlying method Riutort-Mayol et al.[1].

what: What a Gaussian process term does
title: Gaussian process latent terms: maths, priors, and usage
text: gp(time, , kernel, basis_prop) term formula declares smooth latent function time added relevant linear predictor every observation. Writing linear predictor module \(\eta_i\) observation \(\) time \(t_i\) group \(g_i\): \[\eta_i = X_i \beta + Z_i b + f_{g_i}(t_i),\] \(X_i \beta\) fixed effects, \(Z_i b\) random effects (including rw() contributions, handled design matrix), \(f_{g_i}(t_i)\) value Gaussian process group \(g_i\) time \(t_i\). process evolves time axis declared gp(...); observation just looks value (time, group) index. one-dimensional Gaussian processes used zero mean function stationary covariance kernel \(k\), \[f \sim \mathrm{GP}(0,\, k(\Delta t)), \qquad k(t, t') = k(|t - t'|) = k(\Delta t),\] prior correlation two time points depends separation \(\Delta t\). arima() term builds memory step step recursion, Gaussian process places smooth prior whole trajectory , length scale \(\rho\) controls quickly function can change magnitude \(\alpha\) controls far can depart zero.

what: Using Gaussian process terms in a formula
title: Gaussian process latent terms: maths, priors, and usage
text: Add term module’s formula gp(): gp(time) — Gaussian process time default Matérn 3/2 kernel. gp(time, ) — independent realisation per level , sharing length scale magnitude. gp(time, d = 1) — integrated (non-stationary) process (see Differencing order). gp(time, kernel = "se") — different kernel (see Kernels). gp(time, basis_prop = 0.3) — basis functions, trading speed approximation accuracy. time column process evolves , optional gives group realisation shared hyperparameters. latent processes vignette works gp() growth rate compares rw(), arima(), lme4-style random effects; Priors section covers tuning regularisation.

title: Gaussian process latent terms: maths, priors, and usage
text: Gaussian process dense covariance matrix costs \(O(T^3)\) evaluate, prohibitive inside sampler. instead use Hilbert-space reduced-rank (spectral) approximation Riutort-Mayol et al.[1], represents process finite weighted sum fixed basis functions, \[f(t) \approx \sum_{j=1}^{m} \left( S_k(\sqrt{\lambda_j}) \right)^{\frac{1}{2}} \phi_j(t)\, \beta_j,\] \(m\) number basis functions. turns Gaussian process linear model \(m\) standard-normal weights \(\beta_j\), cost \(O(T m)\) rather \(O(T^3)\). number basis functions set proportion \(b\) number time points \(t_\mathrm{GP}\) process applied, rounded [1], \[m = \lceil b\, t_\mathrm{GP} \rceil.\] eigenvalues Laplace operator boundary-scaled interval \[\lambda_j = \left( \frac{j \pi}{2 L} \right)^2,\] \(L\) positive boundary factor, eigenfunctions \[\phi_j(t) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{L}} \sin\!\left( \sqrt{\lambda_j}\, (t^* + L) \right),\] time rescaled linearly lie \([-1, 1]\), \[t^* = \frac{t - \tfrac{1}{2} t_\mathrm{GP}}{\tfrac{1}{2} t_\mathrm{GP}}.\] weights standard-normal prior \(\beta_j \sim \mathcal{N}(0, 1)\), approximation non-centred: kernel’s structure enters spectral densities \(S_k(\sqrt{\lambda_j})\), scale basis function much power kernel places frequency. basis matrix \(\Phi \\mathbb{R}^{T \times m}\) (entries \(\phi_j(t)\)) data-: depend \(\rho\) \(\alpha\), built R side construct_gp() passed Stan data. spectral densities parameter-dependent rebuilt every gradient evaluation. Putting pieces together, latent process one group Stan implementation steps lives inst/stan/functions/gaussian_process.stan, adapted EpiNow2.
code: S    <- diagSPD(alpha, rho, L, M)     # length-M (or 2M) spectral density f    <- PHI * (S .* eta)              # length-T latent, eta ~ Normal(0, 1)

title: Gaussian process latent terms: maths, priors, and usage
text: d argument integrates realisation \(d\) times enters predictor, matching d arima(). controls whether Gaussian process models level latent quantity change. d = 0 (default) stationary process: \(f\) realisation described , fluctuating around zero preferred direction. growth rate corresponds EpiNow2’s gp_on = "R0", process models stationary deviations fixed reproduction number. d = 1 integrates process , increments trajectory smooth rather trajectory . result drifts like random walk correlated, smoothly varying steps, often natural prior epidemic trend. matches EpiNow2’s default gp_on = "R_t-1", Gaussian process placed first difference log reproduction number. d \ge 2 integrates , generalising beyond EpiNow2 smoother non-stationary trends (example \(d = 2\) models curvature smoothly varying slope). Concretely, \(d \ge 1\) spectral approximation generates \(T - d\) free values \(\tilde f\), placed positions \((d+1), \dots, T\) length-\(T\) vector whose first \(d\) entries zero, vector cumulatively summed \(d\) times: \[f = \underbrace{C \cdots C}_{d}\, \begin{pmatrix} \mathbf{0}_d \\ \tilde f \end{pmatrix}, \qquad C \text{ cumulative-sum operator}.\] leading \(d\) entries start zero remain zero every integration pass, \(f_1 = \dots = f_d = 0\). anchoring identifiability fix: leaves free level (\(d = 1\)) additionally free slope (\(d \ge 2\)) module’s fixed effects rather Gaussian process, otherwise confounded intercept. convention EpiNow2 uses non-stationary reproduction-number process (gp[2:(gp_n + 1)] = noise; gp = cumulative_sum(gp)), generalised arbitrary \(d\). Differencing meaningful latent expectation modules (growth rate expr latent--obs proportion expl), drifting non-stationary trend usual model. permitted logit-hazard modules (refnp, rep, miss) API consistency arima(), integrating logit hazard unusual rarely intended model.

title: Gaussian process latent terms: maths, priors, and usage
text: default priors follow EpiNow2[1]. length scale \(\rho\) magnitude \(\alpha\) can overridden standard priors argument relevant module; spectral weights fixed. \(\eta_j \sim \mathcal{N}(0, 1)\) — fixed non-centred parameterisation; user-tunable. \(\alpha \sim \mathcal{N}_+(\mu_\alpha, \sigma_\alpha)\) (half-normal) — controls far process can deviate zero. Set <prefix>_gp_alpha prior. \(\rho \sim \mathrm{LogNormal}(\mu_\rho, \sigma_\rho)\) — controls quickly process can change. Set <prefix>_gp_rho prior. <prefix> module term sits (expr growth rate, refp parametric reference mean, ; see can use ). EpiNow2 defaults chosen scale log reproduction number; given module’s scale (example logit hazard) may need tightening loosening. example, put tighter length scale growth-rate process: magnitude prior \(\alpha\) consequential practice; tighten latent process competing rest linear predictor variance. length-scale prior \(\rho\) lever wiggly function allowed : smaller \(\rho\) permits faster changes, larger \(\rho\) forces smoother trajectory.
code: library(epinowcast) rho_prior <- data.frame(variable = "expr_gp_rho", mean = log(3), sd = 0.25) # epinowcast(..., priors = rho_prior)

title: Gaussian process latent terms: maths, priors, and usage
text: gp() works every module takes formula, prior prefix: enw_expectation() — growth rate (expr) latent--obs proportion (expl). enw_reference() — parametric mean (refp) non-parametric logit hazards (refnp). enw_report() — report-date logit hazards (rep). enw_missing() — missing-reference proportion (miss). Putting gp() term reference delay mean, instance, models reporting delay drifts smoothly time, gp() growth rate models epidemic trend .

title: Gaussian process latent terms: maths, priors, and usage
text: One Gaussian process term per formula. R side aborts multiple supplied. Grouped series share length scale magnitude across groups, gp() take type argument. approximation accuracy controlled basis_prop; basis functions oversmooth short-length-scale structure, many slow fit. code: independent_epinowcast <- function(obs, max_delay = 40, ...) {   pobs_ind <- enw_preprocess_data(obs, max_delay = max_delay)    nowcast <- epinowcast(     data = pobs_ind,     reference = enw_reference(~ rw(week), data = pobs_ind),     report = enw_report(~ (1 | day_of_week), data = pobs_ind),     expectation = enw_expectation(       ~ 0 + (1 | day_of_week) + (1 | day), data = pobs_ind     ),      ...   )   return(nowcast) } options(mc.cores = 2)  independent_nowcast <- map(   split(retro_nat_germany, by = "age_group"),   independent_epinowcast,   fit = enw_fit_opts(     save_warmup = FALSE, output_loglik = TRUE, pp = TRUE,     chains = 2, threads_per_chain = threads,     iter_sampling = 500, iter_warmup = 500,     show_messages = FALSE, refresh = 0,     adapt_delta = 0.95, max_treedepth = 12   ),   priors = priors )  independent_nowcast_summary <- map_dfr(   independent_nowcast,   summary,   probs = c(0.025, 0.05, seq(0.1, 0.9, by = 0.1), 0.95, 0.975) ) enw_plot_nowcast_quantiles(   independent_nowcast_summary, latest_obs = latest_nat_germany ) +   facet_wrap(vars(age_group), scales = "free_y")

code: nowcasts <- list(   "Reference: Fixed, Report: Fixed" = exp_nowcast,   "Reference: Fixed, Report: Day of week" = dow_nowcast,   "Reference: Age, Report: Day of week" = age_nowcast,   "Reference: Age and week, Report: Day of week" = week_nowcast,   "Reference: Age and week by age, Report: Day of week" = age_week_nowcast ) summarised_nowcasts <- map(   nowcasts, summary,   probs = c(0.025, 0.05, seq(0.1, 0.9, by = 0.1), 0.95, 0.975) ) summarised_nowcasts$`Independent by age, Reference: Week, Report: Day of week` <- independent_nowcast_summary # nolint  summarised_nowcasts <- rbindlist(summarised_nowcasts, idcol = "model",                                  use.names = TRUE) summarised_nowcasts[, `:=`(   model = factor(     model,     levels = c("Reference: Fixed, Report: Fixed",                "Reference: Fixed, Report: Day of week",                "Reference: Age, Report: Day of week",                "Reference: Age and week, Report: Day of week",                "Reference: Age and week by age, Report: Day of week",                "Independent by age, Reference: Week, Report: Day of week")),   age_group = factor(     age_group,     levels = c("00+", "00-04", "05-14", "15-34", "35-59", "60-79", "80+")) )] enw_plot_nowcast_quantiles(   summarised_nowcasts, latest_obs = latest_nat_germany ) +   facet_grid(vars(age_group), vars(model), scales = "free_y") loos <- map(nowcasts, \(x) x$fit[[1]]$loo()) loo_compare(loos) #>                                                     elpd_diff se_diff #> Reference: Age, Report: Day of week                    0.0       0.0  #> Reference: Age and week, Report: Day of week          -0.9       5.7  #> Reference: Age and week by age, Report: Day of week   -2.8       4.2  #> Reference: Fixed, Report: Day of week                -86.9      11.4  #> Reference: Fixed, Report: Fixed                     -619.2      46.0 eval_obs <- latest_nat_germany[reference_date > (max(reference_date) - 7)] scores <- map_dfr(   nowcasts, as_forecast_sample, eval_obs, .id = "model" ) |>   rbind(     independent_nowcast |>       map_dfr(as_forecast_sample, eval_obs) |>       within({         model <- "Independent by age, Reference: Week, Report: Day of week"       }),       fill = TRUE   ) |>   as.data.table() |>   within({     .group <- NULL   }) |>   as_forecast_sample() |>   score()   scores |>   summarise_scores(by = "model") |>   summarise_scores(fun = signif, digits = 2, by = "model") |>   kable() relative_scores <- scores |>   add_relative_skill(     by = c("reference_date", "age_group"),     baseline = "Reference: Fixed, Report: Fixed"   ) |>   summarise_scores(by = c("model", "reference_date", "age_group"))   relative_scores <- relative_scores[!model == "Reference: Fixed, Report: Fixed"] plot <- ggplot(relative_scores) +   aes(x = reference_date, y = crps_scaled_relative_skill, col = model) +   geom_hline(yintercept = 1, linetype = 2, linewidth = 1.2, alpha = 0.5) +   geom_line(linewidth = 1.1, alpha = 0.6) +   geom_point(size = 1.2) +   facet_wrap(vars(age_group)) +   scale_color_brewer(palette = "Dark2") +   scale_y_log10(labels = scales::percent)  plot <- enw_plot_theme(plot) +   labs(x = "Reference date",        y = "Weighted interval score (relative to Reference: Fixed, Report: Fixed model)") + # nolint   guides(col = guide_legend(title = "Model", ncol = 2)) plot

title: Comparing Inference Methods
text: epinowcast supports several inference methods CmdStan: NUTS sampling (enw_sample()): default Hamiltonian Monte Carlo sampler. Produces gold-standard posterior samples slowest option. Pathfinder (enw_pathfinder()): fast variational approximation. Useful model development, exploration, quick iteration. Pathfinder-initialised NUTS (enw_sample(init_method = "pathfinder")): uses pathfinder find good starting values NUTS. Can reduce warmup time improve convergence difficult models. vignette compares three approaches across two nowcasting model specifications different complexity. look runtime sampling efficiency, MCMC pathfinder diagnostics, resulting nowcasts, posterior distributions key delay overdispersion parameters. demonstrate real-time workflow uses previous NUTS posterior prior fast pathfinder update, compare pathfinder default priors full NUTS refit show whether informed priors close gap gold-standard reference fraction cost.

title: Comparing Inference Methods
text: use German COVID-19 hospitalisation data package, filtering period (1 July 2021) reporting patterns well behaved demonstrating model fit. Compile model . disable threading single-group models vignette benefit within-chain parallelisation.
code: library(epinowcast) library(data.table) library(ggplot2) library(purrr) # Set a persistent cache for the vignette to avoid re-compilation issues enw_set_cache(file.path(tempdir(), "enw_cache"), type = "persistent") nat_germany_hosp <-   germany_covid19_hosp[location == "DE"][age_group == "00+"] |>   enw_filter_report_dates(latest_date = "2021-07-01")  retro_nat_germany <- nat_germany_hosp |>   enw_filter_report_dates(remove_days = 30) |>   enw_filter_reference_dates(include_days = 30)  latest_germany_hosp <- nat_germany_hosp |>   enw_obs_at_delay(max_delay = 30) |>   enw_filter_reference_dates(remove_days = 30, include_days = 30)  pobs <- enw_preprocess_data(retro_nat_germany, max_delay = 30) model <- enw_model(threads = FALSE)

title: Comparing Inference Methods
text: define two nowcasting specifications share expectation (process), report, observation models differ handle reporting delays. use weekly random walk growth rate day--week effects expected counts, day--week report effects, negative binomial observations (using negbin1d parameterisation linear mean-variance relationship). Spec (static delays): lognormal delays constant time. Spec B (time-varying delays): lognormal delays weekly random walk mean parameter day--week effects, allowing delay distribution change time day week. useful reporting processes evolving, adds parameters. use purrr::partial() create function factory fixes shared settings, need vary reference model fitting options run. also define small helper collect summary() output across named list fits, reuse comparison section.
code: shared_expectation <- enw_expectation(   ~ 1 + rw(week) + (1 | day_of_week), data = pobs ) shared_report <- enw_report(~ (1 | day_of_week), data = pobs) shared_obs <- enw_obs(family = "negbin1d", data = pobs) ref_a <- enw_reference(   parametric = ~1, distribution = "lognormal", data = pobs ) ref_b <- enw_reference(   parametric = ~ 1 + rw(week) + (1 | day_of_week),   distribution = "lognormal",   data = pobs ) fit_nowcast <- partial(   epinowcast,   data = pobs,   expectation = shared_expectation,   report = shared_report,   obs = shared_obs,   model = model )  collect_summaries <- function(fits, type, variables = NULL,                               label_col = "method") {   rbindlist(lapply(names(fits), function(nm) {     args <- list(fits[[nm]], type = type)     if (!is.null(variables)) args$variables <- variables     s <- do.call(summary, args)     s[, (label_col) := nm]     s   })) }

title: Comparing Inference Methods
text: define shared NUTS options tuned speed rather production use. set adapt_delta = 0.80 looked values 0.80 0.95 prior- pathfinder-initialised NUTS fits stayed well 1% divergent-transitions rate every value; running lower end keeps warmup cheap tolerating handful divergences remain. also set max_depth = 10 (Stan default) since none fits hit treedepth cap checks.
code: nuts_args <- list(   save_warmup = FALSE, pp = TRUE,   chains = 2,   iter_sampling = 500, iter_warmup = 500,   adapt_delta = 0.80, max_depth = 10,   show_messages = FALSE, refresh = 0 )

title: Comparing Inference Methods
code: options(mc.cores = 2)  nuts_prior_opts <- do.call(   enw_fit_opts,   c(list(sampler = enw_sample), nuts_args) )  fit_a_nuts <- fit_nowcast(reference = ref_a, fit = nuts_prior_opts) fit_b_nuts <- fit_nowcast(reference = ref_b, fit = nuts_prior_opts)

title: Comparing Inference Methods
text: reuse nuts_args comparison isolates effect initialisation.
code: nuts_pf_opts <- do.call(   enw_fit_opts,   c(     list(sampler = enw_sample, init_method = "pathfinder"),     nuts_args   ) )  fit_a_nuts_pf <- fit_nowcast(reference = ref_a, fit = nuts_pf_opts) fit_b_nuts_pf <- fit_nowcast(reference = ref_b, fit = nuts_pf_opts)

title: Comparing Inference Methods
text: use num_paths = 10 (default 4) improve quality variational approximation subsequent PSIS resampling. Increasing number paths standard way improve pathfinder performance cost additional runtime.
code: pf_opts <- enw_fit_opts(   sampler = enw_pathfinder, pp = TRUE, num_paths = 10 )  fit_a_pf <- fit_nowcast(reference = ref_a, fit = pf_opts) fit_b_pf <- fit_nowcast(reference = ref_b, fit = pf_opts)

title: Comparing Inference Methods
text: Runtime sampling efficiency spec method. Min ESS/s minimum bulk effective sample size per second reported NUTS fits. Note runtime pathfinder-initialised NUTS reported includes NUTS sampling step. pathfinder initialisation additional overhead (though often small compared sampling). plot chunk runtime-plot Pathfinder substantially faster NUTS, completing fraction time. pathfinder initialisation adds overhead NUTS run, pathfinder-initialised NUTS slower standard NUTS models. models NUTS struggles converge (e.g. due complex posteriors poor default initialisation), pathfinder initialisation can reduce warmup iterations overall runtime.
code: fits <- list(   "A: NUTS (prior init)" = fit_a_nuts,   "A: NUTS (pathfinder init)" = fit_a_nuts_pf,   "A: Pathfinder" = fit_a_pf,   "B: NUTS (prior init)" = fit_b_nuts,   "B: NUTS (pathfinder init)" = fit_b_nuts_pf,   "B: Pathfinder" = fit_b_pf )  method_labels <- c(   "NUTS (prior init)", "NUTS (pathfinder init)", "Pathfinder" )  runtime_dt <- data.table(   label = names(fits),   spec = rep(c("A (static delays)", "B (time-varying delays)"), each = 3),   method = rep(method_labels, 2),   runtime = vapply(fits, function(x) x$run_time, numeric(1)) )  ess_per_sec <- vapply(names(fits), function(nm) {   if (grepl("Pathfinder$", nm)) return(NA_real_)   s <- enw_posterior(fits[[nm]]$fit[[1]])   min(s$ess_bulk, na.rm = TRUE) / fits[[nm]]$run_time }, numeric(1)) runtime_dt[, ess_per_s := fifelse(   is.na(ess_per_sec), "-", as.character(round(ess_per_sec, 1)) )]  knitr::kable(   runtime_dt[, .(     Spec = spec, Method = method,     `Runtime (s)` = runtime,     `Min ESS/s` = ess_per_s   )],   digits = 1,   caption = paste(     "Runtime and sampling efficiency for each spec and method.",     "Min ESS/s is the minimum bulk effective sample size per second",     "and is reported only for NUTS fits."   ) ) ggplot(runtime_dt, aes(x = method, y = runtime, fill = spec)) +   geom_col(position = "dodge") +   labs(     x = "Inference method", y = "Runtime (seconds)",     fill = "Specification"   ) +   theme_bw() +   theme(     legend.position = "bottom",     axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 30, hjust = 1)   )

title: Comparing Inference Methods
text: Standard MCMC diagnostics (Rhat, divergent transitions, tree depth) apply NUTS fits. MCMC diagnostics NUTS fits. four NUTS fits converge well: Rhat values close 1 (well 1.05 threshold) divergent transitions minimal (1-2 per fit, handful tolerate discussed ). Reported tree depth sits configured maximum 10; earlier checks max_depth = 12 showed sampler never needed go beyond 10, cap biting. initialisation methods (prior pathfinder) produce comparable convergence behaviour models. production use, aim Rhat < 1.01, zero divergent transitions, tree depths comfortably maximum. See Stan help vignette guidance diagnosing resolving fitting issues.
code: nuts_fits <- list(   "A: NUTS (prior init)" = fit_a_nuts,   "A: NUTS (pathfinder init)" = fit_a_nuts_pf,   "B: NUTS (prior init)" = fit_b_nuts,   "B: NUTS (pathfinder init)" = fit_b_nuts_pf )  diag_dt <- rbindlist(lapply(names(nuts_fits), function(nm) {   f <- nuts_fits[[nm]]   data.table(     Label = nm,     Samples = f$samples,     `Max Rhat` = f$max_rhat,     `Divergent transitions` = f$divergent_transitions,     `Max tree depth` = f$max_treedepth   ) }))  knitr::kable(   diag_dt, digits = 2,   caption = "MCMC diagnostics for NUTS fits." )

title: Comparing Inference Methods
text: extract nowcast summaries fit combine faceted comparison plots. plot three methods one panel per spec posterior medians 90% credible intervals can compared directly. plot chunk nowcast-comparison-plot chunk nowcast-comparison-b spec , three methods produce similar nowcasts good coverage observed data, suggesting pathfinder can acceptable approximation simpler models despite high Pareto k values. spec B, additional parameters time-varying delay model lead greater divergence pathfinder NUTS, reflecting increased difficulty approximation resulting less reliable coverage. results specific model configuration data; always check agreement methods particular setup.
code: nowcast_summaries <- rbindlist(lapply(names(fits), function(nm) {   s <- summary(fits[[nm]], type = "nowcast")   s[, label := nm]   s[, spec := fifelse(     grepl("^A:", nm),     "A (static delays)",     "B (time-varying delays)"   )]   s[, method := gsub("^[AB]: ", "", nm)]   s }))  latest_obs_dt <- data.table(latest_germany_hosp) plot_nowcast_methods <- function(nc_dt, title) {   ggplot(nc_dt, aes(x = reference_date, colour = method, fill = method)) +     geom_ribbon(aes(ymin = q5, ymax = q95), alpha = 0.15, colour = NA) +     geom_line(aes(y = median), linewidth = 0.8) +     geom_point(       aes(y = confirm), colour = "black", fill = "black",       alpha = 0.5, size = 0.8     ) +     geom_point(       data = latest_obs_dt,       aes(x = reference_date, y = confirm),       inherit.aes = FALSE, shape = 2, alpha = 0.5, size = 1     ) +     labs(       x = "Reference date", y = "Notifications",       colour = "Method", fill = "Method", title = title     ) +     theme_bw() +     theme(legend.position = "bottom") } plot_nowcast_methods(   nowcast_summaries[spec == "A (static delays)"],   "Spec A (static delays)" ) plot_nowcast_methods(   nowcast_summaries[spec == "B (time-varying delays)"],   "Spec B (time-varying delays)" )

title: Comparing Inference Methods
text: compare posterior estimates key model parameters across methods. include delay distribution intercepts (refp_mean_int refp_sd_int) overdispersion parameter (sqrt_phi). plot chunk posterior-plot make comparison concrete also tabulate posterior mean 90% credible interval width parameter. Posterior means 90% CrI widths. Across three parameters point estimates (means) broadly aligned NUTS pathfinder. 90% credible intervals pathfinder roughly order magnitude narrower NUTS references — example, refp_mean_int pathfinder width roughly 10% 15% NUTS width. known limitation variational approximations: tend concentrate mode underestimate tail variance, uncertainty raw pathfinder fit trustworthy even central estimates look reasonable. NUTS pathfinder initialisation reproduces NUTS posterior (widths within percent prior-init NUTS), initialisation carry variational bias forward. numbers specific model data — run check setup relying pathfinder posteriors.
code: param_vars <- c("refp_mean_int", "refp_sd_int", "sqrt_phi")  param_summaries <- rbindlist(lapply(names(fits), function(nm) {   s <- summary(     fits[[nm]], type = "fit", variables = param_vars   )   s[, label := nm]   s[, spec := fifelse(     grepl("^A:", nm),     "A (static delays)",     "B (time-varying delays)"   )]   s[, method := gsub("^[AB]: ", "", nm)]   s })) ggplot(   param_summaries,   aes(x = method, y = mean, colour = spec) ) +   geom_pointrange(     aes(ymin = q5, ymax = q95),     position = position_dodge(width = 0.5)   ) +   facet_wrap(~variable, scales = "free_y") +   labs(     x = "Inference method",     y = "Posterior estimate (mean and 90% CrI)",     colour = "Specification"   ) +   theme_bw() +   theme(     legend.position = "bottom",     axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 30, hjust = 1)   ) width_dt <- param_summaries[, .(   Spec = spec, Method = method, Variable = variable,   Mean = round(mean, 2),   `90% CrI width` = round(q95 - q5, 2) )] knitr::kable(width_dt, caption = "Posterior means and 90% CrI widths.")

title: Comparing Inference Methods
text: real-time surveillance model typically refit day new data arrive. Running full NUTS daily expensive. alternative workflow run NUTS obtain high-quality posterior use posterior prior subsequent pathfinder updates, refreshing NUTS fit periodically (e.g. weekly). builds “posterior--prior” approach used Germany age-stratified vignette, uses previous day’s posterior inform next day’s NUTS fit. take step using posterior inform much faster pathfinder update. demonstrate using spec . First, extract posterior summaries spec NUTS fit wide range model parameters. borrow just delay distribution overdispersion parameters, also random effect standard deviations overall intercept give pathfinder update much information possible. epinowcast() merges values default priors via enw_replace_priors(), listed variables updated. simulate “day 2” shifting data window forward one day preprocessing . define helper function using purrr::partial capture shared model specification allowing data updates. build shared day-2 modules pobs_day2 capture partial()-based factory mirroring fit_nowcast . fit day 2 using three approaches: Pathfinder informed priors: cheap daily update. Pathfinder default priors: see informed priors improve approximation. NUTS default priors: gold-standard reference trying match. check whether cheap pathfinder update tracks full NUTS refit, whether using informed priors improves agreement compared running pathfinder scratch. plot chunk update-nowcast-comparison plot chunk update-posterior-comparison posteriors agree closely, cheap pathfinder update informed priors can stand full NUTS refit. Using NUTS-informed priors helps center pathfinder fit posterior mode. may still underestimate tails individual parameters, resulting nowcast often relatively good approximation NUTS gold standard. production workflow might run full NUTS weekly use informed pathfinder updates , falling back full refit parameter agreement drifts. Results model- data-specific: always check setup relying pattern. See ?enw_replace_priors details setting priors previous fits.
code: day1_posterior <- summary(fit_a_nuts, type = "fit") # Borrow key parameters and ensure one row per variable to_borrow <- c(   "refp_mean_int", "refp_sd_int", "sqrt_phi",   "expr_r_int", "expr_beta_sd", "rep_beta_sd" ) day1_posterior <- day1_posterior[   gsub("\\[.*\\]", "", variable) %in% to_borrow ] day1_posterior[, variable := gsub("\\[.*\\]", "", variable)] day1_posterior <- day1_posterior[, .(mean = mean(mean), sd = mean(sd)), by = variable] day1_posterior #>         variable        mean         sd #>           <char>       <num>      <num> #> 1:    expr_r_int -0.01803102 0.11953956 #> 2:  expr_beta_sd  0.22981341 0.09128661 #> 3: refp_mean_int  3.91101934 0.57955965 #> 4:   refp_sd_int  4.14053770 0.24707924 #> 5:   rep_beta_sd  0.41978786 0.13747114 #> 6:      sqrt_phi  1.83300673 0.08172812 retro_day2 <- nat_germany_hosp |>   enw_filter_report_dates(remove_days = 29) |>   enw_filter_reference_dates(include_days = 30)  latest_day2 <- nat_germany_hosp |>   enw_obs_at_delay(max_delay = 30) |>   enw_filter_reference_dates(remove_days = 29, include_days = 30)  pobs_day2 <- enw_preprocess_data(retro_day2, max_delay = 30) fit_day2 <- partial(   epinowcast,   data = pobs_day2,   reference = enw_reference(     parametric = ~1, distribution = "lognormal", data = pobs_day2   ),   expectation = enw_expectation(     ~ 1 + rw(week) + (1 | day_of_week), data = pobs_day2   ),   report = enw_report(~ (1 | day_of_week), data = pobs_day2),   obs = enw_obs(family = "negbin1d", data = pobs_day2),   model = model )  pf_opts_pp <- enw_fit_opts(   sampler = enw_pathfinder, pp = TRUE, num_paths = 10 ) fit_day2_pf_informed <- fit_day2(fit = pf_opts_pp, priors = day1_posterior) fit_day2_pf_default <- fit_day2(fit = pf_opts_pp) fit_day2_nuts <- fit_day2(fit = nuts_prior_opts) update_runtime <- data.table(   Method = c(     "Pathfinder (informed priors)",     "Pathfinder (default priors)",     "NUTS (default priors, full refit)"   ),   `Runtime (s)` = c(     fit_day2_pf_informed$run_time,     fit_day2_pf_default$run_time,     fit_day2_nuts$run_time   ) ) knitr::kable(update_runtime, digits = 1) update_fits <- list(   "Pathfinder (informed priors)" = fit_day2_pf_informed,   "Pathfinder (default priors)" = fit_day2_pf_default,   "NUTS (full refit)" = fit_day2_nuts )  update_nc <- collect_summaries(update_fits, type = "nowcast")  ggplot(   update_nc,   aes(x = reference_date, colour = method, fill = method) ) +   geom_ribbon(aes(ymin = q5, ymax = q95), alpha = 0.15, colour = NA) +   geom_line(aes(y = median), linewidth = 0.8) +   geom_point(     aes(y = confirm), colour = "black", fill = "black",     alpha = 0.5, size = 0.8   ) +   geom_point(     data = data.table(latest_day2),     aes(x = reference_date, y = confirm),     inherit.aes = FALSE, shape = 2, alpha = 0.5, size = 1   ) +   labs(     x = "Reference date", y = "Notifications",     colour = "Method", fill = "Method"   ) +   theme_bw() +   theme(legend.position = "bottom") update_params <- collect_summaries(   update_fits, type = "fit",   variables = c("refp_mean_int", "refp_sd_int", "sqrt_phi") )  ggplot(update_params, aes(x = method, y = mean, colour = method)) +   geom_pointrange(aes(ymin = q5, ymax = q95)) +   facet_wrap(~variable, scales = "free_y") +   labs(     x = NULL, y = "Posterior estimate (mean and 90% CrI)",     colour = "Method"   ) +   theme_bw() +   theme(     legend.position = "bottom",     axis.text.x = element_blank(),     axis.ticks.x = element_blank()   )

Use pathfinder-initialised NUTS standard NUTS shows convergence issues (high Rhat, many divergent transitions) slow warmup. Check Pareto k values pathfinder fits: high values (> 0.7) warn approximation may unreliable. Pareto k values high, try increasing num_paths (e.g., 10 20) improve variational approximation. real-time workflows, consider running full NUTS periodically using pathfinder posterior--prior cheap intermediate updates, shown updating section . computational options see features summary. help Stan diagnostics see Stan help vignette.

title: Latent process and periodic options for the growth-rate model
text: work three German age strata reported nationally five weeks reference dates ending 28 days latest available reports. pobs used every fit difference resulting nowcasts latent-process choice rather data. max_delay = 14 shorter 28–40 days German-data vignettes use. keeps build fast truncates longer reporting tail, especially slower-reporting older age groups, treat nowcasts approximation chosen illustration rather delay tuned data. also build nowcast target: count reference date max_delay (14) days reports accumulated. model trying predict, compare rather absolute latest snapshot, include later reports beyond modelled delay window. reference dates nowcast actually estimates recent, still-incomplete ones; trim comparison data dates first model fit plotted points line nowcast. common fit configuration reused across models: two short NUTS chains, enough read convergence diagnostics without long build. Older age groups tend report slowly, single shared delay distribution misfits groups whose reporting differs average. let delay vary age group shared random effect reuse reference model every fit, comparison stays focused growth-rate choice rather delay.
code: library(epinowcast) library(data.table) #> Warning: package 'data.table' was built under R version 4.5.2 library(ggplot2) # nolint: unused_import_linter. Required by plot.epinowcast(). #> Warning: package 'ggplot2' was built under R version 4.5.2 library(knitr) options(mc.cores = 2) nat_germany_hosp <- germany_covid19_hosp[   location == "DE" & age_group %in% c("00+", "00-04", "80+") ]  retro <- nat_germany_hosp |>   enw_filter_report_dates(remove_days = 28) |>   enw_filter_reference_dates(include_days = 35)  pobs <- suppressWarnings(   enw_preprocess_data(retro, by = "age_group", max_delay = 14) ) latest_obs <- enw_obs_at_delay(nat_germany_hosp, max_delay = 14) fit <- enw_fit_opts(   sampler = enw_sample,   chains = 2, parallel_chains = 2,   iter_warmup = 500, iter_sampling = 500,   adapt_delta = 0.99, max_treedepth = 12,   refresh = 0, show_messages = FALSE, seed = 12345 ) reference_mod <- enw_reference(~ 1 + (1 | age_group), data = pobs)

title: Latent process and periodic options for the growth-rate model
text: rw(week) adds one Gaussian increment per week; cumulative sum increments added r. growth rate drifts smoothly preferred direction mean revert , makes random walk standard non-stationary smoother.
code: nowcast_rw <- epinowcast(   pobs,   expectation = enw_expectation(r = ~ rw(week, age_group), data = pobs),   reference = reference_mod,   obs = enw_obs(family = "negbin", data = pobs),   fit = fit ) nowcast_dates <- unique(summary(nowcast_rw)$reference_date) latest_obs <- latest_obs[reference_date %in% nowcast_dates] plot(nowcast_rw, latest_obs = latest_obs) +   facet_wrap(vars(age_group), scales = "free_y")

title: Latent process and periodic options for the growth-rate model
text: arima(time, , p, d, q) adds latent ARIMA(p, d, q) residual series linear predictor. Differencing applied via cumulative-sum operator ARMA part via parameter-dependent Toeplitz kernel built impulse response; compose single matrix multiplication onto unit-normal shocks. supplied group column unit-normal shocks; AR/MA parameters latent standard deviation currently shared across groups. arima(week, d = 1, p = 0, q = 0) recovers rw(week). Adding p = 1 makes random-walk increments autocorrelated, drift smoother persistent plain random walk remaining non-stationary. model trend weekly resolution, matching rw(week), difference two autoregressive term. ARIMA-specific posterior summaries partial autocorrelation pacf (stationary AR parameterisation) latent standard deviation sigma:
code: nowcast_arima <- epinowcast(   pobs,   expectation = enw_expectation(     r = ~ 1 + arima(week, age_group, p = 1, d = 1),     data = pobs   ),   reference = reference_mod,   obs = enw_obs(family = "negbin", data = pobs),   fit = fit ) plot(nowcast_arima, latest_obs = latest_obs) +   facet_wrap(vars(age_group), scales = "free_y") arima_pars <- summary(nowcast_arima, type = "fit")[   grepl("expr_arima_(pacf|sigma)", variable),   .(variable, mean, q5, q95) ] kable(arima_pars, digits = 3)

title: Latent process and periodic options for the growth-rate model
text: ar(time, , p) alias arima(time, , p = p, d = 0, q = 0). differencing growth rate stationary: fluctuates around fixed mean departures decay rather accumulate. Contrast integrated models , growth rate free wander. Stationary AR right choice growth stable average want model autocorrelated departures level rather drifting trend.
code: nowcast_ar <- epinowcast(   pobs,   expectation = enw_expectation(     r = ~ 1 + ar(day, age_group, p = 1),     data = pobs   ),   reference = reference_mod,   obs = enw_obs(family = "negbin", data = pobs),   fit = fit ) plot(nowcast_ar, latest_obs = latest_obs) +   facet_wrap(vars(age_group), scales = "free_y")

title: Latent process and periodic options for the growth-rate model
text: gp(time, , d, kernel, basis_prop) adds approximate Gaussian process growth rate. random walk AR terms build memory one step next, Gaussian process places smooth prior whole trajectory : nearby time points correlated, length scale model learns data. result flexible smoother can capture curvature random walk reach accumulated noise. default process stationary; gp(week, d = 1) integrates smoothly drifting, random-walk-like trend (mirroring arima()’s d), detailed implementation notes. process fitted using Hilbert-space reduced-rank (spectral) approximation, cost controlled number basis functions rather number time points. basis_prop sets number proportion series length (default 0.2 follows EpiNow2); larger value accurate slower. default kernel = "matern32" Matern 3/2 kernel; "matern52", "ou" (Ornstein-Uhlenbeck), "se" (squared exponential), "periodic" also available. Stan implementation adapted EpiNow2 (MIT licensed). Gaussian process implementation notes cover spectral approximation, kernels, priors detail. Gaussian-process-specific posterior summaries length scale rho (quickly growth rate can change) magnitude alpha (far can depart mean):
code: nowcast_gp <- epinowcast(   pobs,   expectation = enw_expectation(     r = ~ 1 + gp(week, age_group),     data = pobs   ),   reference = reference_mod,   obs = enw_obs(family = "negbin", data = pobs),   fit = fit ) plot(nowcast_gp, latest_obs = latest_obs) +   facet_wrap(vars(age_group), scales = "free_y") gp_pars <- summary(nowcast_gp, type = "fit")[   grepl("expr_gp_(rho|alpha)", variable),   .(variable, mean, q5, q95) ] kable(gp_pars, digits = 3)

title: Latent process and periodic options for the growth-rate model
text: (1 | week:.group) adds separate random level every (week, group) cell, drawn shared Gaussian. (1 | group) notation follows random-effect convention lme4 brms. Unlike time-series terms correlation across weeks; week independent draw. natural choice weekly fluctuations look like noise rather drift.
code: nowcast_re <- epinowcast(   pobs,   expectation = enw_expectation(     r = ~ 1 + (1 | week:.group), data = pobs   ),   reference = reference_mod,   obs = enw_obs(family = "negbin", data = pobs),   fit = fit ) plot(nowcast_re, latest_obs = latest_obs) +   facet_wrap(vars(age_group), scales = "free_y")

title: Latent process and periodic options for the growth-rate model
text: Periodic terms repeat known cycle rather drifting freely. common case surveillance data day--week reporting: weekends look different weekdays roughly way every week. (1 | day_of_week) gives every weekday offset drawn shared Gaussian. Monday offset enters every Monday, Tuesday offset enters every Tuesday, — exactly periodic across weeks. shared Gaussian pools information across seven weekday levels, preferable seven independent fixed effects whenever dataset short. Swap (1 | day_of_week) day_of_week rather unpooled fixed effects.
code: nowcast_dow <- epinowcast(   pobs,   expectation = enw_expectation(     r = ~ 1 + (1 | day_of_week), data = pobs   ),   reference = reference_mod,   obs = enw_obs(family = "negbin", data = pobs),   fit = fit ) plot(nowcast_dow, latest_obs = latest_obs) +   facet_wrap(vars(age_group), scales = "free_y")

title: Latent process and periodic options for the growth-rate model
text: Latent periodic terms compose formula, usual surveillance model: slowly evolving trend plus repeating weekly pattern. integrated AR term carries trend weekly resolution day--week effect captures within-week reporting cycle. term identified explains — trend latent series, weekday structure periodic effect — can read separately fit.
code: nowcast_combined <- epinowcast(   pobs,   expectation = enw_expectation(     r = ~ 1 + (1 | day_of_week) + arima(week, age_group, p = 1, d = 1),     data = pobs   ),   reference = reference_mod,   obs = enw_obs(family = "negbin", data = pobs),   fit = fit ) plot(nowcast_combined, latest_obs = latest_obs) +   facet_wrap(vars(age_group), scales = "free_y")

title: Latent process and periodic options for the growth-rate model
text: ma(time, , q) arma(time, , p, q) expose moving-average part machinery: shock time t influences series lags 0, 1, ..., q drops . moving average sits flat mean, rarely sensible model growth rate — trend short-memory noise sit . MA structure useful component ARMA ARIMA model, captures short-range correlation top autoregressive trend, example arma(day, age_group, p = 1, q = 1) arima(day, age_group, p = 1, d = 1, q = 1). Note also moving average periodic--weekday model: gives correlated noise q + 1 step window, recurring weekly cycle. genuinely periodic within-week variation use day--week term. Higher-order moving averages harder fit: without enforced invertibility constraint MA(q) likelihood multimodal, chains can settle different observationally similar coefficient sets. implementation notes cover invertibility trade-.

title: Latent process and periodic options for the growth-rate model
text: NUTS fits can read standard convergence diagnostics model: largest R-hat smallest bulk effective sample size across parameters, number divergent transitions, total sampler runtime. Values close 1 R-hat, bulk effective sample sizes hundreds, zero divergences indicate short chains converged; , lengthen chains raise adapt_delta. runtime column makes accuracy-versus-cost trade-explicit: flexible latent processes generally cost fit, worth checking extra structure earns keep.
code: fits <- list(   "rw(week, age_group)" = nowcast_rw,   "arima(week, age_group, 1, 1, 0)" = nowcast_arima,   "ar(day, age_group, 1)" = nowcast_ar,   "gp(week, age_group)" = nowcast_gp,   "(1 | week:.group)" = nowcast_re,   "(1 | day_of_week)" = nowcast_dow,   "(1 | day_of_week) + arima(week, ...)" = nowcast_combined ) diagnostics <- rbindlist(lapply(names(fits), function(model) {   fit_summary <- summary(fits[[model]], type = "fit")   divergences <- fits[[model]]$fit[[1]]$diagnostic_summary()$num_divergent   data.table(     model = model,     max_rhat = round(max(fit_summary$rhat, na.rm = TRUE), 3),     min_ess_bulk = round(min(fit_summary$ess_bulk, na.rm = TRUE)),     divergences = sum(divergences),     runtime_s = round(fits[[model]]$fit[[1]]$time()$total)   ) })) #> Warning: The ESS has been capped to avoid unstable estimates. #> Warning: The ESS has been capped to avoid unstable estimates. #> Warning: The ESS has been capped to avoid unstable estimates. #> Warning: The ESS has been capped to avoid unstable estimates. #> Warning: The ESS has been capped to avoid unstable estimates. #> Warning: The ESS has been capped to avoid unstable estimates. #> Warning: The ESS has been capped to avoid unstable estimates. #> Warning: The ESS has been capped to avoid unstable estimates. #> Warning: The ESS has been capped to avoid unstable estimates. #> Warning: The ESS has been capped to avoid unstable estimates. #> Warning: 2 of 1000 (0.0%) transitions ended with a divergence. #> See https://mc-stan.org/misc/warnings for details. #> Warning: 2 of 1000 (0.0%) transitions hit the maximum treedepth limit of 12. #> See https://mc-stan.org/misc/warnings for details. kable(diagnostics)

title: Model definition and implementation

Combined appropriate generation time distribution approach shown correspond Susceptible-Exposed-Infected-Recovered (SEIR) model[7] addition reporting delays. However, default model contains minimal mechanism order flexibly fit highly informative data. nowcasting approach extension proposed Günther et al.[8] extension model proposed Höhle Heiden[9] implemented surveillance R package[10]. Compared model proposed Günther et al.[8], epinowcast adds support jointly nowcasting multiple related datasets, flexible formula interface allowing specification large range models, optional parametric assumption underlying reporting delay. also support flexible joint modelling missing data assuming reporting delay consistent reported unreported observations following methodology Lison et al.[11]. modelling framework implemented stan probabilistic programming language via cmdstanr[12,13] focus computational efficiency robustness. quick reference available features, see model features summary vignette. following sections outline modelling methodology, current feature set stratified module, highlight implementation details. module present default implementation well generic framework support. title: Model definition and implementation
text: concerned outcomes occur time reference (e.g., date symptom onset test disease) reported delay, time report (e.g., date onsets entered central database become available analysis). assume times measured discrete time steps, usually day (case times dates). follow approach Höhle Heiden[9] consider distribution notifications (\(n_{g,t,d}\)) date reference (\(t\)) reporting delay (\(d\)) conditional final observed count \(N_{g,t}\) dataset (\(g\)) , \[\begin{equation}   N_{g,t} = \sum_{d=0}^{D} n_{g,t,d} \end{equation}\] \(D\) represents maximum delay date reference time report theory infinite practice set finite value order make model identifiable computationally feasible. \(t\) \(g\) notifications assumed drawn multinomial distribution \(N_{g,t}\) trials probability vector (\(p_{g,t,d}\)) length \(D\). aim nowcasting predict final observed counts \(N_{g,t}\) given information available time \(t\). estimating components probability vector jointly expected number final notifications (\(\lambda_{g,t} = \mathbb{E}[N_{g,t}]\)) dataset \(g\) time \(t\). alternative approach consider \(n_{g,t,d}\) independently point model can defined regression can fit using standard software appropriate observation model adjustment reporting delay (.e., becomes Poisson Negative Binomial regression). implementation approach available Bastos et al.[14]. downside simplified approach reporting conditionally dependent may make specifying models complex reporting distributions difficult.

title: Model definition and implementation
text: follow approach Günther et al.[8] specify model expected final notifications first order random walk. simple model highly flexible good fit nowcasting problems data highly informative. \[\begin{align}   \log (\lambda_{g,t}) &\sim \text{Normal}\left(\log (\lambda_{g,t-1}) , \sigma^{\lambda}_{g} \right) \\   \log (\lambda_{g,0}) &\sim \text{Normal}\left(\log (N_{g,0} + 1), 1 \right) \\ \sigma^{\lambda}_{g} &\sim \text{Half-Normal}\left(0, 1\right) \end{align}\] \(N_{g0}\), first time point expected observations dataset \(d\), assumed completely observed.

title: Model definition and implementation
Model definition and implementation

title: Model definition and implementation
text: default model, consider reporting delay follow \(\text{LogNormal} \left(\mu^d, \sigma^d \right)\) distribution, parameters \[\begin{align}   \mu^d &\sim \text{Normal} \left(0, 1 \right) \\   \sigma^d &\sim \text{Half-Normal} \left(0, 1 \right). \end{align}\] distribution discretised daily probabilities \(p_{g,t,d}\) (case group \(g\) reference date \(t\) reported delay \(d\)) adjusted maximum delay, see vignette("distributions") details.

title: Model definition and implementation
text: generalise model order support range delay distributions well effects reporting process delay. Following approach Günther et al.[8] others, parameterise delay probability (\(p_{g,t,d}\)) via discrete-time hazard model, .e., \[\begin{equation}   p_{g,t,0} = h_{g,t,0},\ p_{g,t,d} = \left(1 -\sum^{d-1}_{d^{\prime}=0} p_{g,t,d^{\prime}} \right) \times h_{g,t,d}, \end{equation}\] \[ h_{g,t,d} =\text{P} \left(\text{delay}=d|\text{delay} \geq d, W_{g,t,d}\right), \] -called reporting hazard. case group \(g\) reference date \(t\), hazard \(h_{g,t,d}\) states probability reported delay \(d\), given case reported earlier. hazard depends design matrix \(W_{g,t,d}\), encodes baseline delay distribution covariates affect reporting delay. extend model Günther et al. decomposing hazard three components, Parametric baseline hazard \(\gamma_{g,t,d}\): hazard derived parametric delay distribution, parameters depending covariates date reference Non-parametric reference date effect \(\delta_{g,t,d}\): effect hazard depends covariates date reference Non-parametric report date effect \(\epsilon_{g,t,d}\): effect hazard depends covariates date report component adds overall hazard regression logit link \[\begin{equation}   \text{logit} (h_{g,t,d}) = \gamma_{g,t,d} + \delta_{g,t,d} + \epsilon_{g,t,d}, \end{equation}\] maximum delay hazard \(h_{g,t,D}=1\), order enforce assumption observations reported within specified maximum delay. following, describe parameterisation different components.

title: Model definition and implementation
text: parametric baseline hazard \(\gamma_{g,t,d}\) case group \(g\) reference date \(t\) modelled according certain discretised parametric probability distribution parameters \(\mu_{g,t}\) \(\upsilon_{g,t}\). Currently, epinowcast supports three different parametric distributions: () log-normal (default), (ii) exponential, (iii) gamma. distributions discretised adjusted assumed maximum delay using double interval censoring discretisation primarycensored package[15]; see vignette("distributions") details. delay probabilities \(p^{\prime}_{g,t,d}\) obtained discretised delay distribution converted hazards logit scale using \[\begin{equation}   \gamma_{g,t,d} = \text{logit} \left(\frac{p^{\prime}_{g,t,d}}{\left(1 -\sum^{d-1}_{d^{\prime}=0} p^{\prime}_{g,t,d^{\prime}} \right)} \right). \end{equation}\] default case log-normal distribution, parameters \(\mu_{g,t}\) \(\upsilon_{g,t}\) represent log mean log standard deviation. parameter defined using (log-)linear model. model consists intercept number arbitrary, shared covariates, indexed reference date. covariates multiplied fixed (\(\beta_{f,}\)) random (\(\beta_{r,}\)) coefficients (note can include auto-regressive terms), .e., \[\begin{align}   \mu_{g,t} &= \mu_0 + \beta_{f,\mu} X_{\gamma} + \beta_{r,\mu} Z_{\gamma} \\   \text{log} (\upsilon_{g,t}) &= \upsilon_0 + \beta_{f,\upsilon} X_{\gamma} + \beta_{r,\upsilon} Z_{\gamma} \end{align}\] parameters specified via parametric argument enw_reference() (see ?enw_reference), controls \(\mu_{g,t}\) \(\upsilon_{g,t}\) simultaneously. example, enw_reference(parametric = ~ 1 + (1 | age_group)) creates age group-specific random effects parameters , allowing different age groups different delay distributions whilst sharing information hierarchical pooling.
